input.query_abstract: We review theoretical approaches to the understanding of food webs. After an overview of the available food web data, we discuss three different classes of models. The first class comprise static models, which assign links between species according to some simple rule. The second class are dynamical models, which include the population dynamics of several interacting species. We focus on the question of the stability of such webs. The third class are species assembly models and evolutionary models, which build webs starting from a few species by adding new species through a process of "invasion" (assembly models) or "speciation" (evolutionary models). Evolutionary models are found to be capable of building large stable webs.
input.hyp_abstract: We survey theoretical treatments of production networks, in which firms or sectors are joined by directed supplier-customer relationships. Following a summary of empirical input-output tables and firm-level transaction records, we organize the literature into three modeling traditions. The first comprises static generative models, which place directed sourcing links between agents according to simple stochastic rules calibrated to observed degree and trade-weight distributions. The second comprises dynamical models, in which the output levels of coupled producers evolve under nonlinear adjustment equations; here we concentrate on the resilience of the economy's equilibrium, characterizing when a fixed point of aggregate activity is locally stable by linearizing the coupled system and examining the spectrum of its Jacobian. Larger, more densely interlinked economies are generically harder to stabilize, reflecting a complexity-fragility tension. The third tradition builds the network endogenously: firms enter through founding or through imitation of incumbents' input recipes, and a selection filter removes entrants that cannot recover costs, so the architecture self-organizes rather than being imposed exogenously. We show that entry-and-selection processes can assemble large, persistent production structures with realistic layering and supply-cycle statistics, and that the resulting equilibria are markedly more robust than those of comparably sized randomly wired economies. We close by relating cascade-of-failure vulnerability to the generative rules that shaped the underlying topology.
input.candidate_title: Modelling transmission and control of the COVID-19 pandemic in Australia
input.candidate_abstract: There is a continuing debate on relative benefits of various mitigation and suppression strategies aimed to control the spread of COVID-19. Here we report the results of agent-based modelling using a fine-grained computational simulation of the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic in Australia. This model is calibrated to match key characteristics of COVID-19 transmission. An important calibration outcome is the age-dependent fraction of symptomatic cases, with this fraction for children found to be one-fifth of such fraction for adults. We apply the model to compare several intervention strategies, including restrictions on international air travel, case isolation, home quarantine, social distancing with varying levels of compliance, and school closures. School closures are not found to bring decisive benefits, unless coupled with high level of social distancing compliance. We report several trade-offs, and an important transition across the levels of social distancing compliance, in the range between 70% and 80% levels, with compliance at the 90% level found to control the disease within 13--14 weeks, when coupled with effective case isolation and international travel restrictions.
